In the modern corporate world, your former coworkers are your future referrers, clients, and partners. Leaving silently is a massive missed networking opportunity.

The perfect farewell email strikes a balance between professional gratitude and warm nostalgia. It should be sent on your second-to-last day or the morning of your final day. Keep the tone overwhelmingly positive; this is absolutely not the venue to air grievances, mention office politics, or brag about the massive salary bump at your new job. Express genuine appreciation for their support, briefly reminisce about a team victory, and provide a clear avenue for them to contact you in the future, typically via your personal email or LinkedIn.

Use the customized farewell templates below to craft the perfect goodbye. Whether you are blasting a wide network email to a 500-person department or sending an intimate, heartfelt goodbye to your immediate small team, these templates ensure you exit with grace.
templates:
Subject: Thank you and farewell! / [Your Name]

Hi Team,

As some of you may already know, tomorrow will be my last day at [Company Name]. I am moving on to a new opportunity, but I couldn't leave without saying a proper goodbye.

I am incredibly grateful for the past [Number] years I’ve spent working alongside such a talented and driven group of people. I am incredibly proud of what we accomplished together, particularly during the launch of [Massive Project/Win]. 

I have learned so much from all of you, and I will genuinely miss the culture and camaraderie of this team.

I’d love to stay in touch! You can always reach me at [Personal Email] or connect with me on LinkedIn here: [LinkedIn Link].

Wishing you all the absolute best and continued success!

Warmly,
[Your Name]

Subject: Moving on / Thank you for everything

Hi Everyone,

Today is my final day at [Company Name]. 

Before I sign off, I want to say a massive thank you to everyone who made my time here so rewarding. A special shoutout to the [Department Name] team for the endless support, laughs, and hard work over the last few years.

I am incredibly excited for my next chapter, but I am leaving with a heavy heart and nothing but fond memories of my time here.

Please don’t be a stranger! Keep in touch at [Personal Email] or via LinkedIn: [Link].

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Goodbye (for now!) to my favorite team

Hi [Close Team Name],

I couldn't leave without sending a dedicated note to the exact people who made working here so special.

Thank you for always having my back, for the brilliant brainstorming sessions, and for surviving the absolute chaos of the Q4 rollout together. You are brilliant at what you do, and I am a better professional for having worked with you.

As I move on to my next role, I want you to know I am always in your corner. Let’s absolutely grab drinks or coffee soon.

Hit me up anytime at: [Personal Number] or [Personal Email].

I’ll miss you all. Keep crushing it!

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Keep it unfailingly positive. Do not mention any toxicity or bad management in a mass email.
Send a broad, formal email to the wider company, and a highly personalized, casual email to your immediate team.
Always include your personal email AND a link to your LinkedIn profile to make connecting frictionless.

faq.question: When exactly should I send the farewell email?
faq.answer: The afternoon of your second-to-last day, or the morning of your final day. Sending it too early distracts the team, and sending it at 4:55 PM means nobody will see it.

faq.question: Do I have to email the entire company?
faq.answer: No. If you work at a massive enterprise, only email the departments you interacted with daily. Mass emailing 5,000 people can look spammy.

faq.question: Should I mention the name of the new company I am joining?
faq.answer: It's optional. Some professionals mention it ('I am joining Google next week as...'), but simply stating 'I am moving on to a new adventure' is equally professional.

title: Cold Email Templates for SaaS Startup Founders
description: Connect with busy SaaS startup founders using these highly personalized, high-converting cold outreach email templates.
intro:
Pitching a SaaS startup founder is notoriously difficult. Founders are fiercely protective of their time, aggressively filter their inboxes, and possess a sixth sense for generic, automated sales pitches. To successfully capture a founder’s attention, your cold email must be ruthlessly concise, highly relevant to their immediate business goals, and immediately establish your credibility.

The most fatal mistake when emailing a startup founder is focusing on your own product rather than their specific problem. A winning cold email to a founder immediately proves that you understand their current stage of growth. Are they post-Seed and trying to scale outbound sales? Are they struggling with early-stage user retention? Identify a highly specific, painful bottleneck they are likely experiencing and position your solution as the definitive answer. Keep the email under 100 words. Founders respect brevity and directness over lengthy corporate prose.

Use the templates below to structure your founder outreach. Whether you are pitching a new piece of infrastructure software, an agency service, or requesting a partnership, these templates prioritize respect for the founder’s time while maximizing your chances of a positive reply.
templates:
Subject: Scaling [Company Name]'s outbound infrastructure

Hi [Founder Name],

I’ve been following [Company Name] since your recent Seed round announcement—congratulations on the milestone!

As you begin to aggressively scale your sales team this quarter, you will likely hit a massive bottleneck regarding [Specific Problem, e.g., email deliverability and domain reputation]. 

My team at [Your Company] built the exact infrastructure that helped [Similar SaaS Company] solve this, allowing them to scale from 2 to 10 SDRs without hitting spam folders.

Are you open to a brief 10-minute chat next week to see if we can save your team from the same growing pains?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Quick question about [Company Name]'s recent launch

Hi [Founder Name],

I loved the recent Product Hunt launch for [Feature/Product]. It’s a brilliant solution to a problem I actually deal with daily.

I’m reaching out because I noticed that while your core product is phenomenal, the [Specific Area, e.g., user onboarding flow] seems to be causing a slight drop-off in free trial conversions.

I run a specialized agency that helps SaaS founders increase trial-to-paid conversions. I actually recorded a brief 3-minute video showing two immediate tweaks you could make to [Company Name]’s onboarding to boost conversions by roughly 15%.

Would you like me to send over the video link?

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Idea for [Company Name] + [Your Company] partnership

Hi [Founder Name],

I’ll keep this brief. I am the founder of [Your Company], a platform that helps [Target Audience] achieve [Specific Goal].

Given the massive overlap between our user base and [Company Name]’s target market, I believe a co-marketing partnership or native integration could drive significant mutual pipeline for both of our startups this quarter.

If generating low-CAC leads is a priority for you right now, do you have 10 minutes to chat next Tuesday?

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Your thoughts on [Industry Trend]?

Hi [Founder Name],

I’m currently speaking with several leading founders in the [Industry] space regarding the recent shift toward [Specific Trend]. 

As the CEO of [Company Name], your insights on how this will impact [Specific Niche] would be invaluable. I’m putting together a brief report and would love to feature a quote from you.

Would you be open to answering 3 quick questions asynchronously via email? 

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Solving [Company Name]'s [Specific Issue] bottleneck

Hi [Founder Name],

I know you are incredibly busy focused on scaling [Company Name], so I will be direct.

We provide [Service/Software] that specifically helps post-Series A SaaS companies automate their [Specific Process]. We recently helped [Competitor or Similar Company] reduce their [Cost/Time] by [X]% in under 30 days.

If this is a bottleneck your team is currently trying to solve, I’d love to show you a quick 5-minute demo.

Are you available next Thursday afternoon?

Thanks,
[Your Name]
tips: Keep it under 100 words. Founders scan emails on their phones between meetings.
Reference a specific, recent company milestone (e.g., a funding round, a new product launch).
Do not ask them 'Who is the best person to speak with?' A founder wants you to pitch them directly if it involves revenue or growth.

faq.question: Should I mention their competitors in the pitch?
faq.answer: Yes, strategically. Mentioning that you successfully helped a company similar to theirs (or a direct competitor) immediately builds credibility and FOMO.

faq.question: What time should I email a founder?
faq.answer: Early mornings (7 AM - 8 AM) or late evenings (after 6 PM) often yield higher open rates as founders clear their inboxes outside of core meeting hours.

faq.question: Is a Loom video effective for founders?
faq.answer: Extremely. A highly personalized, 2-minute Loom video audit proves you did genuine research and aren't just blasting an automated list.

title: Cold Email Templates to Book SaaS Demos
description: Transform your outbound funnel. Book more qualified software demos with these highly effective SaaS cold email templates.
intro:
Booking a software demo via cold email is the lifeblood of B2B SaaS sales. However, asking a busy professional to sacrifice 30 minutes of their day to watch a software presentation is a massive ask. If your cold email focuses entirely on listing your software's features rather than solving the prospect's urgent problems, your email will be deleted. To successfully book a demo, your email must sell the meeting, not the software.

The most effective demo-booking emails follow a strict framework: establish relevance immediately, agitate a specific pain point, introduce your software as the definitive solution, provide social proof, and end with a frictionless Call to Action (CTA). Do not send a bulleted list of ten different features; focus entirely on the one feature that solves their most painful daily headache. The CTA should be incredibly low-pressure. Instead of aggressively demanding a 45-minute demo, ask if they are open to a brief 10-minute introduction.

Use the customized templates below to supercharge your SDR efforts. Whether you are targeting Enterprise accounts or SMBs, these templates are designed to pique curiosity, establish undeniable value, and seamlessly transition the prospect onto your calendar.
templates:
Subject: Automating [Company Name]'s manual [Process Name] process

Hi [Name],

I imagine your team at [Company Name] spends a massive amount of time manually handling [Specific Process/Task] every week.

We built [Your Software] specifically to eliminate this bottleneck. Our platform automates [Specific Pain Point], allowing teams like yours to save upwards of [X] hours per week and focus on high-value strategy.

We recently helped [Similar Company] completely automate their workflow, resulting in a [X]% increase in operational efficiency.

Are you open to a brief 10-minute walkthrough next week to see how it works?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Quick question regarding your [Specific Software/Stack]

Hi [Name],

I noticed that [Company Name] is currently using [Competitor Tool] to manage your [Specific Operations]. 

While [Competitor Tool] is great for early-stage teams, many [Industry] leaders find it severely lacking when it comes to [Specific Flaw/Missing Feature, e.g., advanced reporting].

[Your Software] was built specifically to solve [Specific Flaw]. We offer robust, automated [Feature] right out of the box, which is why teams like [Client A] and [Client B] recently switched over to us.

Do you have 10 minutes next Tuesday for a quick demo of how the reporting engine works?

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Idea to reduce [Specific Cost/Metric] at [Company Name]

Hi [Name],

As the [Title] at [Company Name], I know that reducing [Specific Metric, e.g., customer churn] is likely a massive priority for your team this quarter.

Most teams try to solve this by [Common Ineffective Solution], which rarely scales. We approach it differently. [Your Software] utilizes [Unique Mechanism] to proactively identify [Pain Point] before it costs you revenue.

If driving down [Specific Metric] is on your radar right now, I’d love to show you a quick 10-minute demo of the platform.

Are you free next Thursday morning?

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: [Company Name] + [Your Company]

Hi [Name],

I’ll keep this incredibly brief. 

We help companies like [Company Name] increase their [Core Metric] by [X]%. 

Our platform, [Your Software Name], plugs directly into your existing infrastructure to automate the tedious parts of [Specific Process].

I’d love to earn 10 minutes of your time to show you a quick demo. If the platform isn’t a fit, I promise to never email you again.

Does next week work for a quick chat?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Following up with a quick 2-minute video

Hi [Name],

Since I know finding time for a full demo is tough, I went ahead and recorded a highly customized, 2-minute walkthrough of how [Your Software] would look integrated specifically with [Company Name]’s currently workflow.

You can watch the quick demo video here: [Link to Loom/Video].

If you see the value in automating [Process], I’d love to schedule a formal 15-minute call to answer any of your technical questions.

Talk soon,
[Your Name]
tips: Sell the meeting, not the software. Your only goal is to pique enough curiosity to get them on a call.
Ask for a 10 or 15-minute meeting. Asking for 30 or 60 minutes in a cold email is an immediate turn-off.
Use strong social proof by name-dropping impressive clients who are in the exact same industry as the prospect.

faq.question: Should I include a calendar link in the first cold email?
faq.answer: Usually, no. Including links in the first cold email can harm deliverability. Ask a 'Yes/No' question first, and send the link when they reply.

faq.question: What if they ask for pricing before booking a demo?
faq.answer: Provide a rough estimate or a starting price to respect their time, but reiterate that the custom demo will allow you to provide an exact, tailored quote.

faq.question: Is it better to attach a PDF deck or do a live demo?
faq.answer: A live demo is infinitely better because it allows you to handle objections in real-time and tailor the presentation dynamically.

title: Cold Email Templates for Real Estate Investors
description: Find off-market deals and distressed properties with these proven cold outreach templates for real estate investors and wholesalers.
intro:
In the highly competitive world of real estate investing, finding lucrative off-market deals requires aggressive, precise outreach. Distressed property owners, absentee landlords, and motivated sellers receive a constant barrage of junk mail and colorful postcards. To stand out and secure the deal, your cold email must be remarkably transparent, professional, and empathetic to their potential situation.

A great real estate outreach email avoids sounding like a scam. Do not use overly aggressive sales language, all-caps, or promises of "Instant Cash." Instead, pitch yourself as a local, professional investor looking to simplify their life. State clearly how you found their property, affirm that you can close quickly with cash (bypassing the headache of traditional financing contingencies), and most importantly, offer to purchase it "as-is" without requiring any repairs or cleaning on their part. This alleviates the massive mental friction associated with selling a distressed asset.

The templates below are tailored for various real estate scenarios, including contacting out-of-state absentee owners, reaching out regarding a pre-foreclosure, or inquiring about a multi-family commercial asset. Customize them with the specific property address to prove you are a serious, local buyer.
templates:
Subject: Inquiry regarding your property at [Property Address]

Hi [Owner's Name],

My name is [Your Name] and I am a local real estate investor here in [City/Area]. 

I am currently looking to purchase a few more investment properties in the [Neighborhood] area this month, and your property at [Property Address] immediately caught my eye.

If you have any interest in selling, I would love to make you a fair, all-cash offer. We purchase properties completely 'as-is'—meaning you won't have to make a single repair, clean the property, or pay any traditional realtor commissions.

We can close on your timeline, whether that is in 14 days or 3 months.

Are you open to a brief 5-minute phone call this week to discuss a potential cash offer?

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Buying [City] rentals — Cash offer for [Property Address]

Hi [Owner's Name],

I noticed through public tax records that you currently own the rental property at [Property Address], but your primary mailing address is out of state.

As a landlord myself, I know how incredibly stressful managing out-of-state properties and dealing with tenant issues can become.

If you are tired of merely breaking even or dealing with the operational headaches, my investment group is actively buying multi-family properties in [City]. We can take the property off your hands quickly, with cash, and we will happily inherit any existing tenants so you don't have to deal with evictions.

If you are interested in exploring an off-market sale, please reply to this email or call me directly at [Your Number].

Thanks,
[Your Name]

Subject: Quick question about [Property Address]

Hi [Owner's Name],

I’ll keep this very brief. I am a local investor heavily buying in the [Zip Code] area. 

I drove by your property at [Property Address] yesterday and I absolutely love the lot. 

If you have ever considered selling, I can offer you a stress-free, all-cash transaction. No open houses, no bank appraisals falling through, and absolutely no repair costs on your end.

Do you have 5 minutes for a quick chat sometime next week?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Interested in selling your commercial building at [Address]?

Hi [Owner's Name],

My investment firm is actively acquiring commercial and mixed-use assets in the [Neighborhood/City] submarket.

Given the current market dynamics, we are prepared to make highly competitive, non-contingent cash offers for properties that fit our criteria. Your building at [Property Address] perfectly aligns with our current Q3 acquisition goals.

We are principals, not brokers, so you would save completely on standard commission fees.

Would you be open to a confidential conversation regarding the valuation of your asset?

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

Subject: Checking in regarding [Property Address]

Hi [Owner's Name],

I reached out a few weeks ago regarding your property at [Property Address], but I know life gets busy.

I am still actively buying in the area and my cash offer stands. If you have been considering offloading the property to free up capital without the headache of a traditional listing, I’d love to be the one to buy it.

Just let me know if you are open to a quick 5-minute exploratory call.

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Always include the exact property address in the subject line. This guarantees high open rates.
Emphasize the primary benefits: 'All Cash', 'As-Is', and 'No Realtor Commissions'.
Ensure your email signature contains your full phone number, website, and physical business address to prove you are legitimate.

faq.question: Where do I find email addresses for property owners?
faq.answer: Investors use 'skip tracing' software (like PropStream or BatchLeads) to cross-reference public property tax records with consumer databases to find phone numbers and emails.

faq.question: Is cold emailing property owners legal?
faq.answer: Yes, standard CAN-SPAM laws apply. Ensure you provide a clear way to opt-out, and do not use deceptive subject lines.

faq.question: What if the property is already listed with an agent?
faq.answer: If it is actively listed on the MLS, you must legally submit your offer through their listing agent, not by cold emailing the seller directly.

title: High-Converting B2B Lead Generation Cold Emails
description: Fill your sales pipeline with qualified B2B leads using these proven, high-converting outbound lead generation email templates.
intro:
Generic, mass-blasted B2B mass emails are dead. Today, decision-makers utilize aggressive spam filters and possess zero tolerance for automated templates that treat them like a number on a spreadsheet. To succeed in modern B2B lead generation, your outbound strategy must shift from purely volume-based approaches to highly targeted, hyper-personalized "spear phishing." A successful B2B cold email must immediately prove that you understand their specific industry constraints and offer a quantifiable, proven solution.

The framework for a perfect B2B lead generation email is: hook, customized observation, social proof, and a low-friction Call to Action. Begin by referencing a recent trigger event—a new round of funding, a recent C-suite hire, or an expansion into a new market. Next, highlight the specific operational pain point that typically accompanies that transition. Provide undeniable social proof by stating exactly how you solved that exact problem for a direct competitor or a similar-sized company in their space. Finally, ask for a brief 10-minute discovery call.

Implement these B2B outbound templates to dramatically increase your positive reply rates. Tailor them for various sectors, from selling enterprise software to pitching high-ticket consulting services, ensuring your messaging resonates deeply with executive buyers.
templates:
Subject: Ideas for scaling [Company Name]'s [Department]

Hi [Name],

I noticed the recent news regarding [Company Name]’s expansion into the [New Market/Region]—congratulations to the team! 

Typically, when companies scale their [Specific Department] that quickly, they run into massive inefficiencies regarding [Specific Pain Point, e.g., manual data entry or compliance tracking].

My team at [Your Company] specializes in eliminating that exact bottleneck. We recently partnered with [Similar Company], helping them automate [Process], which resulted in a [X]% decrease in operational overhead within a month.

Are you open to a brief 10-minute call next Tuesday to see if we can drive similar efficiencies for your new team?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Quick question about [Company Name]'s Q3 goals

Hi [Name],

As the [Title] at [Company Name], I imagine that hitting your aggressive Q3 targets for [Specific Goal, e.g., Lead Generation or Revenue] is currently top of mind.

Most teams in your space rely heavily on [Ineffective Standard Practice], which often yields diminishing returns. We take a completely different approach. Our platform, [Your Product/Service], leverages [Unique Mechanism] to guarantee a [X]% improvement in [Metric].

If improving [Metric] is a priority this quarter, I’d love to share a quick case study on how we achieved this for [Competitor/Similar Brand].

Do you have 10 minutes available next week?

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: A quick resource for your team at [Company Name]

Hi [Name],

I know you are incredibly busy, so I’ll be brief.

I was doing some research on [Company Name]’s current strategy regarding [Specific Area], and I noticed an opportunity to optimize your [Specific Process].

I actually put together a highly-customized, 1-page roadmap detailing exactly how you could implement [Solution] to save roughly [X] hours a week. There is absolutely no pitch attached; I just love what your company is building and wanted to share some value.

Would you like me to send the roadmap over?

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: [Company Name] + [Your Company]

Hi [Name],

I am reaching out because we help companies similar to [Company Name] solve [Specific Problem].

For context, we recently helped [Impressive Client] overhaul their [Process], resulting in a [Huge Tangible Result, e.g., $100k savings or 50% faster closing time].

Our solution integrates directly into your existing workflow without requiring a massive IT overhaul. 

If this sounds like something that could benefit your team this year, I’d love to schedule a brief introductory call.

Are you available next Thursday?

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

Subject: Following up on my previous note

Hi [Name],

I know how quickly inboxes fill up, so I just wanted to bump my previous email to the top of your stack.

If solving [Specific Pain Point] isn’t a priority for [Company Name] right now, absolutely no worries. But if it is on the roadmap for this quarter, I am highly confident we can deliver serious ROI.

Let me know if you’re open to a quick 10-minute chat.

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Personalize the first sentence heavily. Mentioning a specific recent event shows you aren't mass-emailing.
Keep the email focused entirely on the prospect's problems and metrics, not your product's internal features.
A CTA asking 'Are you open to learning more?' often converts better than demanding a specific calendar time.

faq.question: How many emails should I send in a B2B sequence?
faq.answer: A standard highly-targeted B2B outbound sequence consists of 4 to 6 emails spread out over 21 to 30 days.

faq.question: What is an 'Account-Based Marketing' (ABM) approach to cold email?
faq.answer: ABM means targeting multiple specific stakeholders at a single large company simultaneously, tailoring a highly specific message to the CEO, the CTO, and the VP of Sales based on their unique roles.

faq.question: How do I avoid getting sent to spam in B2B outreach?
faq.answer: Always use a customized warmup domain, avoid using too many links or any images in the first email, and verify all email addresses using a tool like ZeroBounce before sending.

title: Cold Emails to Get Digital Marketing Clients
description: Grow your agency rapidly by landing high-ticket clients with these specialized digital marketing cold outreach templates.
intro:
The digital marketing agency space is incredibly saturated. Business owners are bombarded daily with automated emails promising them "Page 1 on Google" or "Guaranteed 10x ROI." Because they have likely been burned by amateur agencies in the past, they view generic marketing pitches with extreme skepticism. To successfully land high-ticket retainer clients, your cold email must break through this skepticism by offering undeniable, customized upfront value before you ever ask for a dime.

The most successful agency cold emails utilize a "Show, Don't Tell" strategy. Do not tell a prospect you are great at SEO; show them a screenshot of their site's broken technical errors. Do not tell them you are a masterful copywriter; rewrite their current Facebook Ad and send the improved version for free in the very first email. By leading with an actionable audit, a custom Loom video, or a tangible strategy document, you instantly position yourself as a consultative expert. You eliminate the risk for the prospect because you have already proven your competence.

Use the specialized templates below to pitch SEO, PPC, social media management, or web design services. Tailor them to highlight the specific revenue the prospect is currently losing due to their marketing gaps.
templates:
Subject: 3 quick ideas to improve [Company Name]'s Google Ads

Hi [Name],
I was doing some market research in [City] and noticed that [Company Name] is currently running paid ads for the keyword '[Specific Keyword]'.

I clicked through your funnel and noticed that the ad points to your generic homepage rather than a highly optimized landing page. In my experience running ads for [Industry], this single mistake typically cuts conversion rates in half, wasting thousands in ad spend.

I put together a quick 3-minute video showing exactly how you can fix this today and instantly lower your Cost Per Lead. 

Would you like me to send the video over?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: [Competitor Name] is outranking you for '[Valuable Keyword]'

Hi [Name],

I run an SEO agency that exclusively helps [Industry] businesses dominate local search.

I ran a quick technical audit on [Company Name]’s website today. While your site design is fantastic, you are currently losing massive organic traffic to [Competitor Name] for the term '[Valuable Keyword]'.

This is largely due to three easily fixable technical errors on your homepage. I mapped out exactly what these errors are and how your current web developer can patch them to regain that traffic.

Are you open to a brief 10-minute chat next week to review the audit?

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: I rewrote [Company Name]'s Facebook Ad for free

Hi [Name],

I saw [Company Name]'s recent Facebook Ad campaign regarding [Product] in my feed today.

As a direct response copywriter, I love the product, but I think the current ad copy is leaving a lot of money on the table by focusing on features rather than the core customer pain point.

I actually took the liberty of rewriting the ad copy for you using a highly-converting framework. I’ve attached it below. Feel completely free to drop this into your Ads Manager today and A/B test it against your current control—no strings attached!

If it ends up lowering your acquisition costs, I’d love to schedule a call to discuss how we can overhaul your entire funnel.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Quick feedback on your website's mobile design

Hi [Name],

I came across [Company Name] while searching for [Service] in [City]. 

Your services look amazing, but when I pulled up the site on my phone, the "Request a Quote" button was broken and the text was misaligned, making it extremely difficult to contact you on mobile.

Since over 60% of local traffic comes from mobile devices, fixing this could immediately result in a massive uptick in leads for you.

I run a web design agency focused on high-conversion redesigns. Would you be open to a 10-minute call to discuss a potential refresh of the site?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Idea for [Company Name]'s email marketing

Hi [Name],

I signed up for your email newsletter last week to learn more about [Company Name]. 

While the content is great, I noticed there wasn't an automated Welcome Sequence in place to nurture new leads like me into paying customers. You are likely missing out on a significant amount of passive revenue.

We build highly automated email flows for ecommerce brands that typically increase revenue by 15-20% on autopilot. 

Do you have 10 minutes next Tuesday to chat about implementing this for [Company Name]?

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Never attack the prospect's current marketing aggressively; they may have designed the website themselves and will take offense.
Provide actionable solutions they can use immediately without hiring you. Counter-intuitively, giving away your secrets makes them want to hire you to execute them.
Personalized Loom videos are the highest converting medium for pitching digital marketing services.

faq.question: Should I attach a pricing sheet in my cold email?
faq.answer: Never. Your goal is to get a discovery call, diagnose their unique problem, and then build a custom high-ticket proposal. Giving prices upfront commoditizes your service.

faq.question: Who is the decision-maker for a marketing pitch?
faq.answer: At small businesses, target the CEO/Owner. At larger companies, target the Director of Marketing or the VP of Growth.

faq.question: How much free value should I offer in the first email?
faq.answer: Offer a 'bite-sized' audit. Point out 1 or 2 specific errors. Do not spend 3 hours doing a complete site overhaul for free before they have even replied.

title: The Perfect Second Follow-Up Email After No Response
description: Learn exactly what to say when a prospect ignores you. Boost your reply rates with these strategic second follow-up email templates.
intro:
Silence is the most common response in professional communication. When an important prospect, hiring manager, or client fails to reply to your initial outreach, it is natural to feel anxious or assume they are entirely uninterested. However, in the vast majority of cases, they simply opened your email while walking into a meeting, mentally noted to reply later, and completely forgot. The second follow-up email is statistically the most critical email in any outbound sequence, often generating higher reply rates than the initial cold email.

The most fatal mistake when sending a second follow-up is sending the dreaded "Just checking in" or "Just bubbling this to the top of your inbox." These phrases provide zero additional value and read as mildly annoying demands for attention. A masterful second follow-up must be a value-add touchpoint. Keep the email incredibly brief (under 3 sentences), pivot slightly from your original angle, and provide a new piece of compelling information—such as a relevant case study, an insightful industry article, or a restructured, lower-friction question.

Use the customized templates below to gracefully re-engage cold prospects. Whether you are following up on a sales pitch, a networking request, or a project proposal, these templates are designed to elicit a response without coming across as desperate or pushy.
templates:
Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

I know how quickly inboxes can back up, so I just wanted to send a quick follow-up to my previous email.

I actually thought of you this morning because we just published a new case study showing exactly how [Similar Company/Competitor] utilized our platform to increase their [Metric] by [X]%. I thought it might be highly relevant to your Q3 goals.

You can read the brief case study here: [Link].

If exploring this is a priority for you right now, let me know if you have 10 minutes next week to chat.

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

I’m reaching back out because I am highly confident that we can help [Company Name] solve your current [Specific Pain Point] bottleneck.

To make things as easy as possible, I recorded a customized, 2-minute video walkthrough showing exactly how our solution would integrate directly into your existing workflow to save you [Time/Money].

Take a quick look here: [Link to Video].

Let me know your thoughts!

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

Hope you are having a great week.

I wanted to quickly bump this thread. Since my last email, we rolled out a massive update to our [Feature Name], making it significantly easier to [Action/Benefit].

Is solving [Specific Problem] still on your roadmap for this quarter, or should I reach back out closer to the end of the year?

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

Just a quick note to follow up on the proposal I sent over last Tuesday.

I understand your team is likely incredibly busy wrapping up the quarter. If you need any additional documentation, clarification on the pricing structure, or references from past clients to help make your decision, please let me know.

Looking forward to hearing from you.

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

Following up on my previous note. 

I totally understand if this isn't a priority for your team right now. If it makes more sense for me to touch base with a different member of the [Department] team regarding this initiative, please let me know who might be best to connect with.

Thanks,
[Your Name]
tips: Always reply in the same email thread to maintain context so they can easily scroll down to read your original pitch.
Wait at least 3 to 4 full business days before sending your second follow-up to avoid appearing overly aggressive.
Provide a specific 'out'. Asking 'Should I pause my outreach for now?' often triggers a highly honest, polite response.

faq.question: Why do people say 'Just checking in' is bad?
faq.answer: It is inherently selfish. It proves you have nothing new or valuable to say, and you are simply demanding they do the work of responding to you.

faq.question: If they don't reply to the second email, is it over?
faq.answer: Not in sales. A standard B2B sales sequence usually encompasses 4 to 6 touches over a month before pausing.

faq.question: Should I change the subject line?
faq.answer: No. Reply directly to your original email string so the subject line simply gains a 'Re:' prefix. This maintains context.

title: The Third Follow-Up Email (Sales & B2B Outreach)
description: Keep the deal alive. Use these third follow-up email sales templates to re-engage silent prospects and push for a definitive answer.
intro:
Sending the third follow-up email in a sales sequence is often a psychological hurdle. By the time a prospect has ignored your initial cold email and your second follow-up, it is easy to assume they are definitively saying "No." However, data proves otherwise. In complex B2B sales cycles, decision-makers are notoriously overwhelmed. They often require three, four, or even five touchpoints before a message finally reaches them at the precise moment they are actively experiencing the problem you solve.

The strategy for a third sales follow-up email is a combination of extreme brevity and a subtle push for a definitive answer. You have already pitched your product and provided value-add content in previous emails; now, you must clarify their stance. The third follow-up is an excellent place to utilize the "Permission to Close" framework. Simply ask if this project has been deprioritized, or if you should temporarily pause your outreach. By giving them permission to say "No," you remove the social friction, which ironically often prompts them to reply with a "Yes, sorry, I've just been swamped!"

Use the tested sales templates below to execute your third touchpoint effectively. These templates focus on generating a definitive "Yes" or "No", saving you from wasting time on dead prospects while successfully re-engaging busy executives.
templates:
Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

I want to respect your inbox, so I'll keep this incredibly brief.

Typically, when I don't hear back, it means that solving [Specific Pain Point] simply isn't a major priority for your team this quarter, or you are currently locked into a contract with another vendor.

Is that the case here? If so, just let me know and I will happily pause my outreach so I stop cluttering your inbox.

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

I know you are incredibly busy, so I'm not looking for a lengthy reply.

Since we last spoke, I actually had my team run a deeper analysis on [Company Name]’s current setup. We identified a potential inefficiency regarding how your team handles [Specific Process] that is likely costing you around [Estimated Dollar Amount] annually.

If you have 5 minutes this week, I’d love to show you our findings. If this isn't a focus area right now, completely understood.

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

Just floating this to the top of your inbox one last time.

If improving your [Metric/Area] isn't on the roadmap for [Company Name] right now, no worries at all. 

Should I reach back out closer to Q4, or is there someone else on the [Department Name] team who is better suited to evaluate [Software/Solution]?

Thanks,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

Hope your week is off to a great start. 

I wanted to circle back regarding my previous emails about overhauling your [Service Area]. I completely understand if this project has been put on the back burner for now.

For future reference, I’m attaching a brief 1-page PDF detailing exactly how we helped [Similar Company] achieve [Result]. 

I will pause my outreach for now, but feel free to reach out whenever timing makes more sense on your end.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Original Subject Line]

Hi [Name],

I’m guessing you’ve been swamped lately, so I’ll be quick.

Are you still open to exploring a solution to automate your [Process Name], or has this initiative been tabled for the year?

A quick reply of 'Yes' or 'No' is all I need!

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Embrace the 'No'. Giving them a polite, guilt-free way to reject you saves both of you time and stops the sequence gracefully.
Keep this email aggressively short. Two or three sentences max.
Never show annoyance or passive-aggression that they haven't replied to your previous emails.

faq.question: Is the third email the 'Break Up' email?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. A third follow-up usually asks for direction. The 'Break Up' email (the final email in a sequence) usually happens at touch 4 or 5.

faq.question: What is the timeline for the third follow-up?
faq.answer: A common cadence is: Email 1 (Day 1), Email 2 (Day 4), Email 3 (Day 9 or 10).

faq.question: Why ask them to reply with just 'Yes' or 'No'?
faq.answer: It removes cognitive load. Telling a busy executive exactly how simple the reply needs to be drastically increases the chances they will actually hit send.

title: Follow Up Email After a Final Interview (No Response)
description: Nervous after a final interview? Use these polite follow-up emails to ask for an update from the hiring manager without sounding desperate.
intro:
Completing a final round interview is an exhilarating milestone, but the subsequent waiting period can be incredibly agonizing. If the hiring manager promised an update by Friday and Monday rolls around with complete silence, panic often sets in. It is crucial to remember that a delayed response rarely signifies a rejection. Hiring managers are juggling internal approvals, budget sign-offs, reference checks, and their own daily responsibilities. The hiring process almost always takes longer than the company initially projected.

When sending a follow-up email after a final interview, your tone must be exceptionally polite, patient, and entirely devoid of frustration. The goal is to reaffirm your profound enthusiasm for the role while gently prompting them for a timeline update. Do not demand an immediate answer. Instead, briefly restate why you are excited about the key challenges discussed in the final interview, ask if they need any further documentation or references from you, and inquire about their updated timeline.

Below are customized templates designed for the post-interview waiting period. Use them to gently nudge the HR team, check in after a missed deadline, or update them professionally if you have received a competing job offer.
templates:
Subject: Checking in - [Your Name] for [Job Title] role

Hi [Hiring Manager's Name],

I hope you’re having a great week.

I’m reaching out to check in on the status of my candidacy for the [Job Title] position. I thoroughly enjoyed our final interview last Tuesday, and the conversations regarding [Specific Project Discussed] only increased my excitement about the potential of joining your team.

Please let me know if you need any additional information, work samples, or references from me to help finalize your decision.

I look forward to hearing an update when you have a moment.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Following up: [Your Name] / [Job Title] interview

Hi [Recruiter/HR Contact],

I hope all is well!

During my final interview last week, the team mentioned they were hoping to make a final decision by [Date passed]. I know how complex the hiring process can be, so I completely understand if that timeline has shifted!

I remain incredibly interested in the [Job Title] role and the opportunity to contribute to the [Specific Department] team.

Could you kindly let me know if there is an updated timeline on your end?

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

Subject: Update on my timeline regarding the [Job Title] role

Hi [Hiring Manager's Name],

I hope your week is going well.

I am reaching out to provide a quick update on my end. I remain incredibly passionate about the [Job Title] position at [Company Name] and truly believe my background is a perfect fit for the challenges we discussed.

However, I recently received a competing job offer from another company. Because [Company Name] remains my absolute first choice, I wanted to respectfully check in on your timeline before I am required to make a final decision by [Deadline Date].

Please let me know if you have any updates on your process.

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Wait at least 2 full business days after their stated deadline before sending a follow-up.
Always reply to the existing email thread with the recruiter or hiring manager to keep the context intact.
If you have a competing offer, transparently stating your decision deadline is a highly effective way to force an immediate response.

faq.question: Who should I email: the recruiter or the hiring manager?
faq.answer: Email the internal recruiter or HR representative first. They manage the timeline. Only email the hiring manager directly if they told you to contact them specifically.

faq.question: What if they don't respond to the follow-up?
faq.answer: Wait one more full week and send a second follow-up. If they ignore two polite check-ins after a final interview, assume they selected another candidate.

faq.question: Does following up make me look annoying?
faq.answer: No. Sending one polite follow-up after a missed timeline demonstrates high professional standards, proactive communication, and genuine interest in the role.

title: Polite Follow-Up Emails After Sending a Proposal
description: Close more deals with these polite, professional follow-up email templates designed to nudge clients after sending a project proposal or quote.
intro:
Sending a customized business proposal, freelance quote, or vendor contract is a massive milestone. However, the momentum often stalls abruptly once you hit send. Business owners and executives are notoriously busy, and while they may be excited about your proposal, reviewing contracts and securing necessary budget approvals always takes a backseat to their daily operational fires. Leaving the deal entirely in their hands is a massive mistake; a professional follow-up is required to push the deal across the finish line.

A masterful follow-up email to a proposal must walk a delicate line: it must be proactive without being pushy. The most effective strategy is to assume that the delay is caused by complexity, not rejection. Reach out with a helpful, consultative tone. Instead of demanding to know if they have signed it yet, ask if they require any points of clarification, if they need adjustments to the scope of work to better fit their budget, or if they need additional materials to present to their board.

Use the customized templates below to gracefully follow up on lingering quotes, proposals, and contracts. These refined scripts will help you maintain your professional leverage while maintaining positive momentum to close the deal.
templates:
Subject: Checking in regarding the [Project Name] proposal

Hi [Client Name],

I hope you are having a fantastic week!

I’m reaching out to softly check in on the proposal I sent over last [Day of week] regarding the [Project Name] initiative.

I know reviewing scopes of work can be incredibly time-consuming. Have you had a chance to review the document with your team? Please let me know if you have any questions regarding the project phases or if you need any adjustments to the current timeline to better fit your Q3 goals.

I’m happy to hop on a quick 10-minute call later this week if that is easier for you.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: Proposal for [Company Name]

Hi [Client Name],

Hope all is well.

Following up on the quote I provided for the upcoming [Service Type] project. Since I know these decisions often require internal alignment, I wanted to see if you needed any additional information from me to present to the leadership team?

I’m also happy to hop on a quick call to walk through the pricing structure or adjust the scope if budget constraints have shifted since we last spoke.

Let me know your thoughts when you have a moment.

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Next Steps — [Project Name]

Hi [Client Name],

Just floating this email back to the top of your inbox.

I am incredibly excited about the potential of partnering with [Company Name]. To ensure we can hit your desired launch date of [Desired Date], we would ideally need to finalize the contract and begin the onboarding phase by [Deadline Date].

Is your team still on track to move forward this month, or has the timeline for this initiative shifted?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: Contract for [Project Name]

Hi [Client Name],

I hope you’re having a great week.

I’m reaching out because I want to ensure my team is fully prepped and allocated for your upcoming project.

Have you had a chance to review the contract I sent over? If there are any specific clauses or deliverables you would like to discuss or tweak before signing, please let me know and I will gladly send over a revised version today.

Looking forward to getting started!

Sincerely,
[Your Name]
tips: Create urgency by mentioning deadlines. If they have a specific launch date, remind them that signing the proposal is necessary to meet it.
Offer to modify the scope. Sometimes a client 'ghosts' because the price was too high and they are embarrassed to negotiate.
Keep a consultative tone. Assume they are busy, not actively ignoring you.

faq.question: How soon after sending a proposal should I follow up?
faq.answer: Wait roughly 3 to 4 business days. Give them adequate time to read the document and discuss it internally.

faq.question: What if the client says they are 'still reviewing it'?
faq.answer: Reply politely acknowledging their timeline, and ask 'Is it okay if I check back in next Wednesday?' This secures permission for your next follow-up.

faq.question: Should I offer a discount in the follow up to close the deal?
faq.answer: No. Never negotiate against yourself. Wait for them to state their objections to the price before offering to adjust the scope or provide a discount.

title: LinkedIn Message to Hiring Manager After Applying
description: Stand out from the crowd. Send these proven LinkedIn messages to hiring managers immediately after applying for a job.
intro:
Applying for a job through a standard online portal often feels like sending your resume into a black hole. Modern Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) automatically filter out hundreds of highly qualified candidates before human eyes ever see them. To bypass this automated filtering and guarantee your application is reviewed, you must proactively reach out to the hiring manager directly on LinkedIn immediately after submitting your formal application.

A direct message to a hiring manager is not a venue to paste your entire cover letter. It must be aggressively concise, respectful, and highly strategic. A winning LinkedIn outreach message states clearly that you just formally applied, highlights your one or two most impressive, highly relevant metrics, and reiterates your profound enthusiasm for the company's mission. By reaching out directly, you demonstrate exceptional initiative, proactive communication skills, and intense interest in the specific role—traits every hiring manager values.

Utilize the optimized LinkedIn connection requests and direct message templates below to follow up on your recent applications. Customize them to highlight specific intersections between your past achievements and exactly what is requested in their job description.
templates:
Subject: [Job Title] Application / [Your Name]

Hi [Hiring Manager's Name],

I hope you’re having a great week! 

I’m reaching out because I just formally applied for the open [Job Title] position via your careers portal. I have been following [Company Name]’s recent expansion into the [Specific Industry] space and I am incredibly passionate about the work your team is doing.

Given my extensive background in [Skill 1] and my recent success scaling [Specific Project] by [X]%, I strongly believe I could deliver immediate impact to your team.

I understand you are incredibly busy reviewing applications, but I would be honored to briefly discuss how my skill set aligns with your current goals. 

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Connecting regarding the [Job Title] role

Hi [Hiring Manager's Name],

I noticed on LinkedIn that you are the hiring manager for the [Job Title] role. I just submitted my formal application this morning.

Because I am incredibly passionate about solving [Specific Problem the Company Solves], I wanted to personally reach out. In my previous role at [Previous Company], I successfully managed a very similar challenge, resulting in a [Huge Tangible Metric—e.g., $5M revenue increase].

I would love the opportunity to bring that same strategic rigor to your team.

Thank you for your time, and I look forward to potentially connecting. 

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Connection Request (Note) 

Hi [Hiring Manager],

I just applied for the [Job Title] role! Given my 5+ years of driving [Specific Result] in the [Industry] space, I’m highly confident I can help the team tackle the upcoming Q3 goals mentioned in the job description. Would love to connect and keep up with your team's work!

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Exploring the [Job Title] role at [Company Name]

Hi [Recruiter/Hiring Manager Name],

I see your team is currently recruiting for a [Job Title]. I just submitted my resume through the portal but wanted to introduce myself directly.

My background is heavily anchored in [Skill A] and [Skill B]. Notably, I recently led a team to [Specific Achievement], which I noticed is closely aligned with the core responsibilities of this open role.

I’d welcome the chance to briefly hop on a call to see if my background aligns with what you’re looking for currently.

Best,
[Your Name]
tips: Keep it brief. Hiring managers read LinkedIn messages on their phones; get to the point instantly.
If you are sending a Connection Request note, you are limited to 300 characters. Make every word count.
Always apply through the formal portal first so the HR team has your data properly logged.

faq.question: How do I find the hiring manager on LinkedIn?
faq.answer: Search for '[Company Name] + [Department Leader Title]' (e.g., 'Director of Engineering' if you are applying for a developer role).

faq.question: Should I attach my resume via LinkedIn DM?
faq.answer: Yes, attaching your resume as a PDF directly in the message makes it incredibly frictionless for them to review it instantly.

faq.question: What if they read it but don't reply?
faq.answer: Mission accomplished. The goal was to ensure they looked at your profile and pulled your resume from the ATS system. Give them time to process the application.

title: LinkedIn Recruiter Follow-Up Messages
description: Keep the conversation going. Master the art of the LinkedIn recruiter follow-up message to secure your next interview.
intro:
Following up with a recruiter on LinkedIn after an initial screen, a final interview, or simply after sending your resume requires extreme tact. Recruiters represent the gatekeepers to your target company; managing an enormous pipeline of candidates daily means they frequently fall behind schedule. A pushy, demanding LinkedIn direct message will rapidly relegate your application to the "No" pile. Conversely, a highly professional, polite nudge demonstrates excellent communication skills and intense interest in the role.

The perfect LinkedIn follow-up message to a recruiter must be brief, gracious, and empathetic to their heavy workload. Start by explicitly referencing your previous interaction—whether it was a phone screen last Tuesday or simply an application you filed yesterday. Briefly reiterate exactly why your core experience aligns with their open position, providing a single, specific metric or skill as proof. Conclude by respectfully asking for a timeline update without demanding an immediate decision.

Use the customized LinkedIn DM and InMail templates below to gracefully follow up with recruiters across various stages of the hiring pipeline. These proven scripts help ensure your name remains top-of-mind without seeming desperate.
templates:
Subject: Following up: [Your Name] for [Job Title]

Hi [Recruiter's Name],

I hope your week is off to a great start!

I’m reaching out to follow up on my application for the [Job Title] role. Since we connected last [Day/Week], I’ve been researching [Company Name]’s recent pivot into [Specific Strategy/Market], which makes me even more excited about the opportunity.

Given my 5+ years of experience leading similar [Specific Skill] initiatives at [Previous Company], I strongly believe my background closely aligns with the team's Q3 goals.

Could you kindly let me know if there is an updated timeline regarding next steps in the interview process?

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Checking in - [Job Title] role

Hi [Recruiter Name],

Hope all is well.

I completely understand how busy your pipeline must be right now! I simply wanted to check in regarding the [Job Title] position we discussed during our phone screen last Tuesday.

I remain highly interested in joining the team and helping scale the [Specific Department]. 

Please let me know if the hiring manager requires any additional references or work samples from me to make their decision. Looking forward to hearing from you!

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Your Name] / [Job Title] candidacy

Hi [Recruiter's Name],

I am writing to provide a quick update on my end regarding the [Job Title] position.

I remain incredibly passionate about the opportunity to join [Company Name], specifically because of the amazing culture you described during our last call.

However, I recently advanced to the final stage with another organization. Because [Company Name] remains my absolute top choice, I wanted to respectfully check in on your timeline to see if we might be able to coordinate final next steps before [Deadline Date].

Hope to connect soon,
[Your Name]

Subject: Great speaking with you today! 

Hi [Recruiter Name],

Thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today about the [Job Title] role. 

Learning more about the team's immediate focus on [Specific Challenge] solidified my interest in the position. As discussed, I successfully managed a similar transition at [Previous Company], resulting in a [Huge Tangible Metric].

I’ve attached my updated portfolio here for your reference. I look forward to hearing about the next steps by the end of the week!

Cheers,
[Your Name]
tips: Wait at least 48 hours after their promised deadline before gently checking in.
LinkedIn DMs do not require formal 'Dear Sir' greetings; keep the tone professional but slightly conversational.
If you have a competing offer, transparently stating your deadline is the best way to force an immediate timeline update.

faq.question: Should I message the recruiter or email them?
faq.answer: Reply using whatever platform they used to contact you last. If they scheduled the call via email, reply to that email thread. If they reached out via LinkedIn InMail, reply there.

faq.question: What if the recruiter ignores my follow-up?
faq.answer: Recruiters are often waiting on hiring managers. Wait another full week, send one final polite message. If there is still no response, assume the role was filled.

faq.question: Is it okay to connect with the recruiter after applying?
faq.answer: Yes. Sending a customized connection request ('Hi [Name], I just applied for the [Role] and would love to stay connected regarding future opportunities') is highly proactive.

title: LinkedIn Cold Outreach for Business Partnerships
description: Forge powerful B2B alliances. Use these LinkedIn cold outreach templates to secure lucrative business partnerships and co-marketing deals.
intro:
Securing a strategic B2B partnership or co-marketing agreement can immediately unlock a massive new audience for your business without the hefty customer acquisition costs associated with paid ads. However, executives guarding these audiences receive dozens of "partnership" requests weekly that are thinly veiled sales pitches. To successfully secure a highly leveraged business partnership on LinkedIn, your cold outreach must clearly, instantly articulate the mutual benefit (the "What's In It For Me" or WIIFM).

A brilliant partnership pitch focuses entirely on synergy and audience overlap. Do not begin by talking extensively about your own product's features. Instead, compliment their recent work, identify that both of your companies target the exact same customer persona (e.g., "e-commerce founders doing $1M+ ARR"), and propose a straightforward, low-lift collaboration—such as a co-hosted webinar, a guest newsletter take-over, or a native software integration. Your goal is to drastically lower the perceived risk and effort required on their end.

Use the tested LinkedIn InMail and Connection Request templates below to initiate these high-value conversations. Because LinkedIn constraints require brevity, these scripts prioritize impactful metrics, explicit synergy, and an incredibly frictionless call to action.
templates:
Subject: Partnership idea: [Your Company] + [Their Company]

Hi [Name],

I’ve been following [Company Name]’s recent growth and absolutely love the new [Feature/Product] you launched last month. 

I am the [Title] at [Your Company]. Since we both strictly serve [Target Audience Persona, e.g., B2B SaaS Founders], I believe there is a massive opportunity for a mutually beneficial co-marketing partnership this quarter.

We currently have an active email list of [Number] highly engaged founders. I would love to explore hosting a joint [Webinar/Co-authored E-book] to cross-pollinate our audiences and drive incredibly low-CAC leads for both of our sales teams.

Are you open to a brief 10-minute exploratory chat next Tuesday?

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Quick idea regarding a native integration

Hi [Name],

As a massive fan of [Company Name], I wanted to reach out directly.

My team at [Your Company] provides [Specific Service/Software] to over [Number] clients in the [Industry] space. The number one request we consistently receive is 'Does this integrate seamlessly with [Company Name]?'

I strongly believe formally integrating our platforms could drastically increase retention for both of our user bases by solving [Shared Pain Point].

If exploring strategic product partnerships is a priority for you right now, I’d love to show you a quick 3-minute mockup of how this could work.

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Connection Request (Note)

Hi [Name],

Since [Company A] and [Company B] both cater heavily to [Target Audience], I’d love to connect. We’re currently looking for trusted partners in the [Niche] space to refer our excess inbound leads to.

Would be great to network and explore potential synergies down the line. Keep up the great work!

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Idea for a Guest Masterclass with [Company Name]

Hi [Name],

I absolutely loved your recent LinkedIn post outlining [Specific Topic]. Your framework for [Specific Detail] is brilliant.

We host a monthly Masterclass for our community of [Number] [Target Audience, e.g., real estate agents]. I would be honored to have you or a member of your leadership team come on as a guest expert to teach that exact framework.

It’s a great, low-lift opportunity to organically pitch [Company Name]’s services to a highly engaged, vetted audience.

Let me know if this sounds interesting and I can send over the details!

Best regards,
[Your Name]
tips: Quantify your audience size (e.g., 'an active list of 10,000 developers') to instantly prove you bring tangible value to the partnership.
Pitch a specific, low-friction idea (like a webinar or a blog swap) rather than a vague 'Let's partner'.
Ensure your own LinkedIn profile is optimized and looks deeply professional before sending high-level partnership pitches.

faq.question: What makes a 'good' partnership?
faq.answer: The golden rule is audience overlap without direct competition. You should target the exact same customer persona, but sell entirely different, complementary products.

faq.question: Who handles partnerships at a large company?
faq.answer: Look for titles like 'Director of Strategic Partnerships', 'Head of Business Development', or 'VP of Marketing'.

faq.question: Should I ask for a referral fee immediately?
faq.answer: No. Focus on audience cross-pollination first. Once trust is established, negotiate formal affiliate structures or revenue shares.

title: Thank You Email After Job Rejection Templates
description: Keep the door open. Learn exactly how to write a classy thank you email after a job rejection to secure future opportunities.
intro:
Receiving a job rejection email after numerous rounds of interviews is incredibly demoralizing. The instinct is to immediately delete the email and move on. However, sending a professional, gracious "thank you" email after a rejection is a masterclass in emotional intelligence and long-term networking. Hiring managers frequently keep the resumes of stellar "runner-up" candidates; when their first-choice candidate declines the offer or suddenly quits three months later, the runner-up who sent a classy thank you email is the very first person they call.

A brilliant post-rejection email serves three distinct purposes. First, it graciously thanks the hiring team for their time, proving extremely high character. Second, it briefly reiterates your continued passion for the company's mission. Third, it clearly asks them to keep your resume on file for future openings within that specific department. By maintaining a supremely professional tone despite the disappointment, you transition from a rejected applicant into a highly valued connection within the hiring manager’s network.

Use the templates below to strategically respond to a rejection. These customized emails will help you leave a lasting, positive impression, keeping the door wide open for future roles, freelance projects, or referrals.
templates:
Subject: Re: [Job Title] role - [Your Name]

Hi [Hiring Manager's Name],

Thank you so much for getting back to me and letting me know about your decision.

While I am naturally disappointed I won’t be joining the team at this time, I truly appreciate the opportunity to interview for the [Job Title] position. I thoroughly enjoyed our conversations regarding [Specific Project or Topic], and the process only reinforced my deep admiration for [Company Name]’s mission.

I would love to stay connected as you continue to scale the [Department Name] team. Please keep my resume on file for any future opportunities where my background in [Skill A] and [Skill B] might be a strong fit.

Wishing you and the entire team the absolute best with the upcoming Q3 product launch.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Thank you and keeping in touch / [Your Name]

Hi [Recruiter's Name],

Thank you for the update. While I am certainly bummed to hear the news, I genuinely appreciate your transparency and the incredibly smooth interview process you facilitated.

I remain incredibly passionate about the work [Company Name] is doing in the [Industry] space. If a similar [Job Title/Department] position opens up in the future, I would be thrilled to be considered again.

Thank you again for your time, and I wish the team great success moving forward.

Cheers,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Job Title] update

Hi [Hiring Manager Name],

Thank you for reaching out to let me know the team’s decision.

It was a privilege to learn more about the incredible culture you’ve built at [Company Name]. I especially appreciated your insights on [Specific Challenge discussed in interview]—it gave me a lot to think about regarding my own career growth in [Industry].

I’ve sent you a connection request on LinkedIn. I look forward to following the team's continued success, and I hope our paths cross again in the future!

Warmly,
[Your Name]
tips: Reply within 24 hours while your candidacy is still fresh in their minds.
Send a connection request to the hiring manager on LinkedIn immediately after sending the email.
Keep it genuinely polite; absolutely no passive-aggression or demands to know exactly why you were rejected.

faq.question: Should I ask for feedback in this email?
faq.answer: You can, but it is often better to ask in a separate 'Feedback Request' email or combine it very softly: 'If you have a brief moment to share any feedback on areas I could improve, I would greatly appreciate it.'

faq.question: Do runner-ups really get hired later?
faq.answer: Absolutely. The #1 candidate often falls through during salary negotiations or quits rapidly. Hiring managers hate re-opening job reqs; they prefer to call the polite runner-up immediately.

faq.question: Who should receive the email?
faq.answer: Reply directly to the person who delivered the rejection (usually HR), but explicitly ask them to pass your gratitude along to the specific Hiring Manager.

title: How to Ask for Feedback After an Interview (Templates)
description: Turn a "No" into a growth opportunity. Use these polite, professional email templates to ask for actionable feedback after a job rejection.
intro:
Asking for feedback following a job rejection is one of the most proactive steps you can take to accelerate your career growth. While many companies possess strict HR policies against providing detailed interview feedback (to avoid potential liability), hiring managers will occasionally share invaluable, candid critique if your request is remarkably humble and professional. This feedback can highlight a blind spot in your interview presence or a specific technical gap you need to close before applying for your next role.

The key to a successful feedback request is removing all pressure. The hiring manager must know that you are not attempting to argue with their decision or litigate your rejection. State clearly that you accept their decision fully, and explicitly note that you are asking purely for your own professional development. Ask for one or two highly specific bullet points rather than a comprehensive performance review. Make it as easy as possible for them to reply safely and quickly.

Below are customized templates designed to extract constructive criticism gracefully. Whether you are asking the internal recruiter or the direct hiring manager, these scripts maximize your chances of receiving actionable advice while cementing your reputation as a dedicated professional.
templates:
Subject: Re: Update on the [Job Title] role

Hi [Hiring Manager's Name],

Thank you again for letting me know about your final decision regarding the [Job Title] role. I truly appreciate your transparency and the time your team spent with me.

Since I am constantly looking for ways to improve my professional skill set, I was hoping to ask a brief favor. If you have 2 minutes to spare, I would immensely appreciate any brief, candid feedback regarding my interview performance or any specific gaps in my [Skillset, e.g., technical background] compared to the successful candidate.

I completely accept your decision and am strictly looking for constructive critique to help my continued career growth.

Thank you again for your time and leadership.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

Subject: Re: [Job Title] Application Feedback

Hi [Recruiter's Name],

Thank you for providing the update. While disappointed, I genuinely enjoyed the rigorous interview process and remain a massive fan of [Company Name].

As I continue my job search within the [Industry] sector, I would love to ask if the hiring team shared any specific feedback regarding where my candidacy fell short.

Whether it was a lack of experience in [Software] or simply a culture fit, any brief pointers you could share would be incredibly valuable for my future interviews.

Thank you so much for your guidance throughout this entire process.

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Gathering feedback / [Your Name]

Hi [Interviewer's Name],

Thank you for the update on the [Job Title] position.

I am currently looking to aggressively upskill in [Industry Field] over the next six months. Because I deeply respect your technical expertise in this area, your objective feedback would mean the world to me.

If you have a brief moment, could you share one or two areas where you felt my technical assessment could have been stronger?

I completely understand if company policy prevents you from sharing specifics. Regardless, I wish you and the team a fantastic rest of the year.

Cheers,
[Your Name]
tips: State explicitly that you accept their decision. This lowers their defense mechanism and makes them feel safe providing critique.
Ask a specific question (e.g., 'Was my technical presentation lacking?'). Generic questions yield generic corporate answers.
Never respond defensively. If they provide critique, reply only with a sincere 'Thank you so much for this insight'.

faq.question: Why do companies often refuse to give feedback?
faq.answer: Many corporate HR departments expressly forbid hiring managers from giving feedback to avoid any risk of discrimination lawsuits if the candidate misinterprets the critique.

faq.question: Should I ask for a phone call to get feedback?
faq.answer: No. Asking for a call is a massive time commitment. Allow them to send two quick bullet points asynchronously via email.

faq.question: Is it worth asking if I didn't make it past the first round?
faq.answer: Usually not. First-round rejections are often purely based on resume metrics. Only ask for feedback if you completed later-stage, intensive interviews.

title: Asking for a Job Referral Email Templates
description: Bypass the resume black hole. Learn how to ask a connection for a job referral or an introduction to the hiring manager with these templates.
intro:
Employee referrals are the single highest-converting source of hires for virtually every major corporation. Applying through the standard online portal means competing against hundreds, sometimes thousands, of applicants. An internal referral guarantees your resume skips the initial automated ATS screening and lands directly on the recruiter’s desk. However, asking a professional connection to vouch for you is a significant request; they are putting their own internal reputation on the line.

When writing an email to ask for a referral, your primary objective is to make the process completely frictionless for your contact. Do not ask them "Hey, are there any jobs open at your company?" You must do the heavy lifting beforehand. Identify the exact open job requisition ID, write a brief paragraph outlining exactly why you are hyper-qualified, and attach your polished resume. By providing them with a "forwardable email," they simply click forward, add a quick "Hey HR, see my friend below," and the referral is complete.

The customized templates below cover various referral scenarios, from asking a close former colleague to requesting a referral from a loose LinkedIn acquaintance. Use them to dramatically increase your interview rate and access the hidden job market.
templates:
Subject: Quick question regarding the [Job Title] role at [Company Name]

Hi [Connection's Name],

I hope you’re having a great week! I saw the recent news about [Company Name]'s Series B—huge congratulations to the team.

I’m reaching out because I noticed your team just opened a requisition for a [Job Title] (Req ID: #1234). I actually successfully managed a very similar [Specific Project] during my time at [Previous Company], and I strongly believe my deep expertise in [Skill] makes me a perfect fit for this role.

Would you be comfortable submitting my resume as an internal referral for this position, or connecting me directly with the hiring manager?

I’ve attached my updated resume and a brief 'forwardable' paragraph below to make it as easy as possible. Absolutely no pressure at all if you aren't comfortable doing so!

Best,
[Your Name]

Subject: Forwardable: [Your Name] for [Job Title] role

Hi [Contact's Name],

Hope everything is going great over in the [Department]!

As we’ve discussed previously, I’m actively looking to transition into a [Target Role] position, and [Company Name] is at the absolute top of my list. I saw the [Job Title] role was just posted today.

If you believe I’d be a strong cultural add to the team, would you be open to passing my resume along to the recruiting team? 

I pasted a brief blurb below you can easily forward. Let me know if you need any other info from me. I really appreciate your support!

Cheers,
[Your Name]

---
FORWARDABLE BLURB:
Hi Team,
I’d love to refer my former colleague, [Your Name], for the [Job Title] role. They have 5+ years of experience in [Skill] and recently led [Specific Massive Win]. I loved working with them and strongly believe they’d be a massive asset to our team. I’ve attached their resume for review.

Subject: Connecting regarding an open role at [Company Name]

Hi [Name],

It’s been great following your career since our time together at [Previous Company]!

I’m reaching out because I am preparing to apply for the [Open Role] at [Company Name]. Since you know my work ethic firsthand from our time collaborating on the [Project Name], I was hoping you might be willing to act as an internal referral for me.

Would you be open to a quick 5-minute chat next week to discuss what the hiring manager is primarily looking for in this role?

Hope to catch up soon!

Warmly,
[Your Name]
tips: Always provide a 'Forwardable Blurb'. This allows the employee to refer you in 30 seconds rather than having to draft an email themselves.
Include the exact Job Link and the Requisition ID number so HR knows exactly which role you are applying for.
Offer an 'out'. Saying 'No pressure if you don't feel comfortable' preserves the relationship if they decline.

faq.question: Should I apply online first or ask for the referral first?
faq.answer: Usually, ask for the referral first. Many companies have an internal portal where the employee must submit your resume before you formally apply so they get their referral bonus.

faq.question: Is it okay to ask for a referral from someone I barely know?
faq.answer: It is high-risk. Employees are staking their internal reputation on you. It is better to ask them for an 'informational interview' first to build trust.

faq.question: How much is an employee referral bonus?
faq.answer: Many tech and corporate companies pay employees $1,000 to $5,000 for successful engineering or senior hires, meaning they are financially motivated to refer strong candidates.

title: Email to Schedule a Meeting with a CEO or Executive
description: Get past the gatekeepers. Learn how to write highly effective emails to schedule a meeting with a CEO, President, or senior executive.
intro:
Getting a meeting on a CEO or senior executive’s calendar requires navigating the highest levels of corporate defense. Executive Assistants act as ruthless gatekeepers, explicitly tasked with protecting the CEO’s time from low-value interruptions.
